title: SEGMENT 13 — The ESKAPE pathogens: the WHO priority list

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — The ESKAPE pathogens: the WHO priority list
If you want one mnemonic to carry away from this lecture about which bacteria you should worry about, it is ESKAPE, an acronym coined in a 2008 paper by Louis Rice.
It stands for the six bacterial species most responsible for hospital-acquired multidrug-resistant infections worldwide.
The WHO's 2017 priority pathogen list and the CDC's threat reports both center on essentially these six.
E — Enterococcus faecium.
Vancomycin-resistant enterococcus, particularly the faecium species (which is more resistant on average than faecalis).
Hospital-acquired bloodstream and surgical-wound infections in immunocompromised patients.
S — Staphylococcus aureus.
MRSA, with the small but real threat of VRSA.
Skin, soft tissue, bloodstream, endocarditis, post-surgical.
K — Klebsiella pneumoniae.
CRE, particularly KPC- and NDM-producing strains.
Pneumonia, urinary tract, bloodstream, often in ICU patients with central lines.
A — Acinetobacter baumannii.

Carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, an environmental gram-negative that has become a major problem in ICUs and in combat-zone hospitals (it has a remarkable affinity for war-injury wounds, leading to the nickname "Iraqibacter" during the Iraq war).
Often resistant to virtually all available antibiotics.
P — Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

The intrinsically multi-resistant gram-negative that combines all four resistance mechanisms — efflux pumps, beta-lactamases, porin loss, target modification — and that haunts cystic fibrosis patients, burn units, and ventilator-associated pneumonia.
E — Enterobacter species.
Hospital-acquired gram-negative infections, often with inducible AmpC beta-lactamases and increasingly with carbapenemases.
These six species together account for the majority of multidrug-resistant healthcare-associated infections in the developed world.

They are not the only resistant pathogens that matter — Mycobacterium tuberculosis is its own enormous story, Neisseria gonorrhoeae is on the CDC's urgent threat list as drug-resistant strains spread, Clostridioides difficile is in some ways a special case of antibiotic-induced disease — but the ESKAPE acronym captures the core of the hospital-acquired resistance problem in a single mnemonic.

Six species, responsible for most of the resistance-attributable mortality, all featuring some combination of the four resistance mechanisms we built in Segment 3 and all sharing genes via the horizontal gene transfer mechanisms of Segment 8.
Memorize ESKAPE.
It's the shape of the problem.
